item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You turned the tired trope of journal entries (which I have twice used myself) into something utterly haunting

This is a perfect example of the narrative leading with the anomaly being merely a tool to expand on a theme.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title of this article: is it a reference to that famous quote "The greatest trick the Devil ever pulled was convincing the world he didn't exist"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first impression seeing the page … that chemical structure, where did I see it before? My first thought was caffeine, certainly looks similar. Now that would make for a nice shock at the end, the amnestic being distributed in coffee. No, doesn't quite match.. theobromine? Not that either. Wait, xanthine? Yup. Isn't that present in nearly all lifeforms? Now that is even weirder than it being caffeine, and invites a whole lot of speculation - perhaps the Foundation bioengineered us to produce and/or respond to xanthine so we can forget things naturally?

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Amnestics are such common place in scp articles and rarely the focus, this manages to make a simple common topic into something interesting, and that's awesome to me. The story about the family is down to earth and relatable which contrasts nicely with the overall implications of faulty amnestics. I don't have much negative to say, the only thing would be the ending could've been more clear but overall this was an easy +1, great job.


Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to DrAkimoto or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the article as an exploration into faulty amnestics, although I didn't quite get the ending.

I have some doubts about the compound - it is described as acid-based, but from my understanding of chemistry and the similarities to purine is probably should be base-based if not basic (as in antonim to acidic).
